trajectory_summary: Norm arrived pre-closed, stated his ROI conclusion twice, and Tanner accepted it both times without a single reframe attempt. The bill figure was captured but never used. The orphaned_flow was initiated and abandoned. The call ended in a soft exit that leaves no path back. This is a missed reframe opportunity on a pre-closed lead — not a catastrophic failure of rapport, but a complete absence of the one move that could have changed the outcome.
outcome_dispute: The v1 outcome tag is listed as unknown. Based on the transcript, the correct tag is disengaged_no_appointment — Tanner explicitly closes with 'you know where to call,' which is a soft exit with no booked or tentative appointment and no locked callback time. This is not a DQ (Norm is a homeowner with existing solar and a residual bill) — it is a missed reframe that ended in voluntary disengagement.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Objection inversion — using the customer's own bill figure as the flip instrument
primary_coaching_focus.quote: the return on investment, you know, to get another five or 10 panels may drive the cost down to zero, but it would take me twenty years to pay for them. So, you know, financially, it doesn't make sense to me.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Tanner had the bill number ($100–$150 with a pool and spa) in hand from 00:53 and never used it. The ROI objection appeared twice and was bypassed both times. This is the single highest-leverage coaching gap on this call: Tanner needs to understand that the bill figure is not just a qualification data point — it is the emotional anchor for the entire reframe. The manager should walk through what the flip sounds like using Norm's own numbers, then ask Tanner to practice it.

key_moments.quote: Hey there, Norm. This is Tanner with Ion Solar. How are you doing today? I'll be super quick here. I'm just reaching out to you because you filled out that form online about solar options for your home.
key_moments.stewart_read: Tanner's intro is clean — company name stated, form reference included, human pause invited. This is the one structural beat Tanner executes correctly. It is worth noting because the rest of the call does not build on this foundation.

key_moments.quote: I don't see there's a financial benefit to making the investment. We already have 11 panels.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the call's hinge moment. Norm delivers a reasoned ROI objection — not an emotional one — and Tanner bypasses it entirely to ask about the bill. Strategic read: Tanner may have been gathering data before reframing. Reactive read: Tanner had no reframe ready and defaulted to the next script step. The reactive read is supported by the fact that the bill figure captured at 00:53 was never used as leverage — if Tanner were setting up a flip, the bill number would have been the instrument. It was not.

key_moments.quote: Okay. But that's because we got a swimming pool and a spa.
key_moments.stewart_read: Norm volunteers the pool and spa detail as a defensive explanation — he is neutralizing the bill figure before Tanner can use it, essentially saying 'the bill is high but it's not a solar problem, it's a load problem.' This is not an enthusiasm signal in the buying-signal sense; it is a customer pre-empting the pitch. However, the residual load is real: a pool, a spa, and 11 existing panels still producing a $100–$150 monthly bill is a meaningful anchor. Tanner does not use it.

key_moments.quote: No. I when I when I got online, I was just looking to see if New Mexico still gave, tax reduction form like we did last year.
key_moments.stewart_read: Norm reveals that the form fill was not a solar purchase inquiry — it was a tax credit search. This reframes the entire lead: Norm was not shopping for solar, he was looking for a government incentive and landed on Ion's form. Tanner's response ('those have been expired since, like, I believe, the first of the year') is a knowledge-gap moment — the rep is confirming a policy detail with hedged language ('I believe') rather than redirecting to the specialist. More importantly, Tanner does not use this revelation to reframe the appointment as the place to get accurate, current incentive information.
